GDP and life expectancy globally between countries over the years

null

2026-06-15

Thea von Sydow 2882066

Set-up your environment

Title Page

A new sentence Include your names

Tutorial group: 3.6

Tutorial lecturer: Chantal Schouwenaar

Part 1 - Identify a Social Problem

1.1 Describe the Social Problem

Differences in life expectancy between countries are one of the clearest forms of global inequality. A child born in a high-income country can expect to live around fifteen to twenty years longer than a child born in a low-income country, and this gap has persisted for decades. The classic explanation links population health to national income: (Preston, 1975) showed that life expectancy rises steeply with GDP per capita at low income levels and flattens among richer countries, which implies that economic growth buys the most health where countries are poorest (Deaton, 2003)]. A key lever for policy is therefore knowing how tightly health follows income, because that determines whether health gaps should be fought with economic development, with health spending, or both.

What is less well established is how this relationship behaves during a global shock. The COVID-19 pandemic caused the first worldwide drop in life expectancy in decades, and the losses were very unequal between countries (Schöley, 2022), yet income alone did not fully predict who lost the most. In this report we quantify the relationship between GDP per capita and life expectancy for ten countries across all income levels between 2019 and 2023, and we examine how life expectancy changed around the 2020 outbreak of the COVID-19 pandemic.

Part 2 - Data Sourcing<

2.1 Load in the data

Preferably from a URL, but if not, make sure to download the data and store it in a shared location that you can load the data in from. Do not store the data in a folder you include in the Github repository!

midwest is an example dataset included in the tidyverse package

2.2 Provide a short summary of the dataset(s)

In this case we see 28 variables, but we miss some information on what units they are in. We also don’t know anything about the year/moment in which this data has been captured.

library(readr)
API_NY_GDP_PCAP_PP_KD_DS2_en_csv_v2_1700 <- read_csv("API_NY/API_NY.GDP.PCAP.PP.KD_DS2_en_csv_v2_1700.csv", 
    skip = 3)
## New names:
## Rows: 266 Columns: 71
## ── Column specification
## ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────── Delimiter: "," chr
## (4): Country Name, Country Code, Indicator Name, Indicator Code dbl (35): 1990,
## 1991, 1992, 1993, 1994, 1995, 1996, 1997, 1998, 1999, 2000, ... lgl (32): 1960,
## 1961, 1962, 1963, 1964, 1965, 1966, 1967, 1968, 1969, 1970, ...
## ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data. ℹ
## Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
## • `` -> `...71`
###View(API_NY_GDP_PCAP_PP_KD_DS2_en_csv_v2_1700)

#change data name:
library(readr)

gdp_raw <- read_csv(
  "API_NY/API_NY.GDP.PCAP.PP.KD_DS2_en_csv_v2_1700.csv",
  skip = 3
)
## New names:
## Rows: 266 Columns: 71
## ── Column specification
## ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────── Delimiter: "," chr
## (4): Country Name, Country Code, Indicator Name, Indicator Code dbl (35): 1990,
## 1991, 1992, 1993, 1994, 1995, 1996, 1997, 1998, 1999, 2000, ... lgl (32): 1960,
## 1961, 1962, 1963, 1964, 1965, 1966, 1967, 1968, 1969, 1970, ...
## ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data. ℹ
## Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
## • `` -> `...71`
###View(gdp_raw)

# cleaning the data for 10 countries and 5 years
library(dplyr)

gdp_selected <- gdp_raw %>%
  select(
    country = `Country Name`,
    country_code = `Country Code`,
    `2019`,
    `2020`,
    `2021`,
    `2022`,
    `2023`
  )


#Filter selected countries
selected_countries <- c(
  "USA",
  "NLD",
  "DEU",
  "JPN",
  "CHN",
  "IND",
  "BRA",
  "ZAF",
  "NGA",
  "AUS"
)

gdp_10countries <- gdp_selected %>%
  filter(country_code %in% selected_countries)



saveRDS(gdp_10countries, "gdp_10countries.rds")

Please use a separate ‘R block’ of code for each type of cleaning. So, e.g. one for missing values, a new one for removing unnecessary variables etc.

library(readr)
Life_Expectancy_raw_data <- read_csv("API_SP/API_SP.DYN.LE00.IN_DS2_en_csv_v2_265357.csv")
## New names:
## • `` -> `...3`
## Warning: One or more parsing issues, call `problems()` on your data frame for details,
## e.g.:
##   dat <- vroom(...)
##   problems(dat)
## Rows: 268 Columns: 3
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## Delimiter: ","
## chr (3): Data Source, World Development Indicators, ...3
## 
## ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
## ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
#changing the name of data life expectancy

life_raw <- read_csv("API_SP/API_SP.DYN.LE00.IN_DS2_en_csv_v2_265357.csv", skip = 3)
## New names:
## Rows: 266 Columns: 71
## ── Column specification
## ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────── Delimiter: "," chr
## (4): Country Name, Country Code, Indicator Name, Indicator Code dbl (65): 1960,
## 1961, 1962, 1963, 1964, 1965, 1966, 1967, 1968, 1969, 1970, ... lgl (2): 2025,
## ...71
## ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data. ℹ
## Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
## • `` -> `...71`
#filtering 5 years for life expectancy

library(tidyverse)
library(dplyr)

life_select <- life_raw %>%
  select(
    country = "Country Name",
    country_code = "Country Code",
    "2019",
    "2020",
    "2021",
    "2022",
    "2023")


#filtering 10 countries for life expectancy

selected_countries <- c(
  "USA",
  "NLD",
  "DEU",
  "JPN",
  "CHN",
  "IND",
  "BRA",
  "ZAF",
  "NGA",
  "AUS"
)

life_expectancy_10countries <- life_select %>%
  filter(country_code %in% selected_countries)


saveRDS(life_expectancy_10countries, "life_expectancy.rds")
#merge the data and make the plots
#install.packages("tidyverse")
library(tidyverse)

gdp_10countries <- readRDS("gdp_10countries.rds")

life_expectancy_10countries <- readRDS("life_expectancy.rds")
##View(gdp_10countries)


#Convert GDP data to long format
gdp_long <- gdp_10countries %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = `2019`:`2023`,
    names_to = "year",
    values_to = "gdp_per_capita"
  )

#Convert life expectancy data to long format
life_long <- life_expectancy_10countries %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = `2019`:`2023`,
    names_to = "year",
    values_to = "life_expectancy"
  )

#combine 2 data sets
combined_data <- gdp_long %>%
  left_join(
    life_long,
    by = c("country", "country_code", "year")
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    year = as.numeric(year)
  )

saveRDS(
  combined_data,
  "combined_data.rds"
)
#plotting average of 10 countries life expectancy
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

# Calculate average life expectancy per year
avg_life <- combined_data %>%
  group_by(year) %>%
  summarise(
    avg_life_expectancy = mean(life_expectancy, na.rm = TRUE)
  )
# Plot
ggplot(avg_life, aes(x = year, y = avg_life_expectancy)) +
  geom_line(color = "darkgreen", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Average Life Expectancy of 10 Countries (2019–2023)",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Average Life Expectancy"
  )

ggsave("average_life_expectancy.png", width = 8, height = 5, dpi = 300)

#explanation:Average life expectancy across the ten selected countries declined from approximately 75.3 years in 2019 to 74.0 years in 2021. It subsequently recovered and reached approximately 75.5 years in 2023. The decline coincides with the COVID-19 pandemic period, although this descriptive figure alone cannot establish a causal relationship.
#Spatial variation visualised

combined_data <- readRDS("combined_data.rds")
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(sf)
## Warning: package 'sf' was built under R version 4.5.2
## Linking to GEOS 3.13.0, GDAL 3.8.5, PROJ 9.5.1; sf_use_s2() is TRUE
library(rnaturalearth)
## Warning: package 'rnaturalearth' was built under R version 4.5.2
library(rnaturalearthdata)
## 
## Attaching package: 'rnaturalearthdata'
## The following object is masked from 'package:rnaturalearth':
## 
##     countries110
library(countrycode)
## Warning: package 'countrycode' was built under R version 4.5.2
library(scales)
## 
## Attaching package: 'scales'
## The following object is masked from 'package:purrr':
## 
##     discard
## The following object is masked from 'package:readr':
## 
##     col_factor
combined_data <- readRDS("combined_data.rds")

latest_year <- max(combined_data$year, na.rm = TRUE)

map_data <- combined_data %>%
  filter(year == latest_year) %>%
  mutate(
    iso3 = countrycode(
      country,
      origin = "country.name",
      destination = "iso3c"
    )
  )

world <- ne_countries(
  scale = "medium",
  returnclass = "sf"
)

world_gdp <- world %>%
  left_join(
    map_data,
    by = c("iso_a3" = "iso3")
  )

country_labels <- world_gdp %>%
  filter(!is.na(gdp_per_capita)) %>%
  st_point_on_surface()
## Warning: st_point_on_surface assumes attributes are constant over geometries
## Warning in st_point_on_surface.sfc(st_geometry(x)): st_point_on_surface may not
## give correct results for longitude/latitude data
gdp_map <- ggplot(world_gdp) +
  geom_sf(
    aes(fill = gdp_per_capita),
    linewidth = 0.15
  ) +
  geom_sf_text(
    data = country_labels,
    aes(
      label = paste0(
        name_long,
        "\nLE: ",
        round(life_expectancy, 1),
        " years"
      )
    ),
    size = 2.5,
    check_overlap = TRUE
  ) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(
    labels = label_dollar(
      accuracy = 1,
      big.mark = ","
    ),
    na.value = "grey90",
    name = "GDP per capita\n(US$)"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = paste0(
      "GDP per Capita Across 10 Selected Countries (",
      latest_year,
      ")"
    ),
    subtitle = "Map colours indicate GDP per capita; labels show life expectancy",
    caption = "Source: cleaned and merged dataset. Authors' own calculations."
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    axis.title = element_blank(),
    axis.text = element_blank(),
    panel.grid = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "right"
  )

gdp_map
## Warning in st_point_on_surface.sfc(sf::st_zm(x)): st_point_on_surface may not
## give correct results for longitude/latitude data

dir.create("output", showWarnings = FALSE)

ggsave(
  "gdp_per_capita_map.png",
  plot = gdp_map,
  width = 13,
  height = 7,
  dpi = 300
)
## Warning in st_point_on_surface.sfc(sf::st_zm(x)): st_point_on_surface may not
## give correct results for longitude/latitude data

3.5 Visualize sub-population variation

The graph highlights a clear difference between developed and developing countries. Developed countries generally show both higher GDP per capita and higher life expectancy, whereas developing countries tend to have lower values for both indicators. This pattern suggests that higher levels of economic development are associated with better health outcomes and longer lives.

library(tidyverse)

setwd("~/Documents/GitHub/Group-6-assignment")

combined_data <- readRDS("combined_data.rds")

glimpse(combined_data)
## Rows: 50
## Columns: 5
## $ country         <chr> "Australia", "Australia", "Australia", "Australia", "A…
## $ country_code    <chr> "AUS", "AUS", "AUS", "AUS", "AUS", "BRA", "BRA", "BRA"…
## $ year            <dbl> 2019, 2020, 2021, 2022, 2023, 2019, 2020, 2021, 2022, …
## $ gdp_per_capita  <dbl> 58048.619, 57260.465, 58327.415, 60029.133, 60684.960,…
## $ life_expectancy <dbl> 82.90000, 83.20000, 83.30000, 83.20000, 83.05122, 75.8…
subpop_data <- combined_data %>%
  mutate(
    development_group = ifelse(
      gdp_per_capita >= 20000,
      "Developed countries",
      "Developing countries"
    )
  )
ggplot(subpop_data, aes(
  x = gdp_per_capita,
  y = life_expectancy,
  color = development_group
)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.8) +
  labs(
    title = "GDP per Capita and Life Expectancy by Development Group",
    subtitle = "Comparison between developed and developing countries",
    x = "GDP per capita",
    y = "Life expectancy",
    color = "Development group",
    caption = "Source: World Bank indicators"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

  ggsave(
  "sub_population_analysis.png",
  width = 8,
  height = 5,
  dpi = 300
)

5.2 Reference list

Cutler, D., Deaton, A., & Lleras-Muney, A. (2006). The determinants of mortality. Journal of Economic Perspectives, 20(3), 97–120. https://doi.org/10.1257/jep.20.3.97

Deaton, A. (2003). Health, inequality, and economic development. Journal of Economic Literature, 41(1), 113–158. https://doi.org/10.1257/002205103321544710

Preston, S. H. (1975). The changing relation between mortality and level of economic development. Population Studies, 29(2), 231–248. https://doi.org/10.1080/00324728.1975.10410201

Schöley, J., Aburto, J. M., Kashnitsky, I., Kniffka, M. S., Zhang, L., Jaadla, H., Dowd, J. B., & Kashyap, R. (2022). Life expectancy changes since COVID-19. Nature Human Behaviour, 6, 1649–1659. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41562-022-01450-3

World Health Organization. (2023). World health statistics 2023: Monitoring health for the SDGs. https://www.who.int/publications/i/item/9789240074323
